canonical_value: The single phase equivalent circuit of a three-phase, four pole induction motor is shown in Figure 1. For the purposes of this problem we will assume armature resistance is negligible. The machine is connected to a three-phase voltage source with line-neutral voltage of 200 volts RMS (346 volts line-line) and a frequency of 400 radians/second. The motor reaches peak torque at a speed of 160 radians/second (about 1528 RPM). What is the value of that peak torque (in N-m)?

The motor reaches peak torque at a speed of 160 radians/second (about 1528 RPM). What is the value of that peak torque (in N-m)?

normalized_value.title: EEE-Bench 开放题

The motor reaches peak torque at a speed of 160 radians/second (about 1528 RPM). What is the value of that peak torque (in N-m)?

The forward voltage of the zener diode VZ is 0.7V, and the reverse breakdown voltage is 5V. Find the output voltage \(U_O\). Give the final value to one decimal place. The output voltage \(U_O =\) ________ V.

surface_forms: The figure illustrates a cascaded system of two water tanks.

canonical_value: - into the first tank at a rate \( r_0(t) \),

canonical_value: - out of the first tank and into the second at a rate \( r_1(t) \), and
surface_forms: - out of the first tank and into the second at a rate \( r_1(t) \), and

canonical_value: The rate of flow out of each tank is proportional to the height of the water in that tank: \( r_1(t) = k_1 h_1(t) \) and \( r_2(t) = k_2 h_2(t) \), where \( k_1 \) and \( k_2 \) are each \( 0.2 \, \text{m}^2/\text{second} \).
surface_forms: The rate of flow out of each tank is proportional to the height of the water in that tank: \( r_1(t) = k_1 h_1(t) \) and \( r_2(t) = k_2 h_2(t) \), where \( k_1 \) and \( k_2 \) are each \( 0.2 \, \text{m}^2/\text{second} \).

normalized_value.text: The rate of flow out of each tank is proportional to the height of the water in that tank: \( r_1(t) = k_1 h_1(t) \) and \( r_2(t) = k_2 h_2(t) \), where \( k_1 \) and \( k_2 \) are each \( 0.2 \, \text{m}^2/\text{second} \).
Both tanks have heights of 1m.

canonical_value: The cross-sectional area of tank 1 is \( A_1 = 4 \, \text{m}^2 \) and that of the second tank is \( A_2 = 2 \, \text{m}^2 \).
surface_forms: The cross-sectional area of tank 1 is \( A_1 = 4 \, \text{m}^2 \) and that of the second tank is \( A_2 = 2 \, \text{m}^2 \).

canonical_value: At time \( t = 0 \), both tanks are empty.
surface_forms: At time \( t = 0 \), both tanks are empty.

surface_forms: Assume that \( r_0(t) \) is held constant at rate \( r_0 \).

canonical_value: What is the maximum value of \( r_0 \) such that neither tank will ever overflow if both tanks start out empty?
surface_forms: What is the maximum value of \( r_0 \) such that neither tank will ever overflow if both tanks start out empty?

normalized_value.target_text: What is the maximum value of \( r_0 \) such that neither tank will ever overflow if both tanks start out empty?

canonical_value:
The figure illustrates a cascaded system of two water tanks. Water flows:
- into the first tank at a rate \( r_0(t) \),
- out of the first tank and into the second at a rate \( r_1(t) \), and
- out of the second tank at a rate \( r_2(t) \).
The rate of flow out of each tank is proportional to the height of the water in that tank: \( r_1(t) = k_1 h_1(t) \) and \( r_2(t) = k_2 h_2(t) \), where \( k_1 \) and \( k_2 \) are each \( 0.2 \, \text{m}^2/\text{second} \). Both tanks have heights of 1 m. The cross-sectional area of tank 1 is \( A_1 = 4 \, \text{m}^2 \) and that of the second tank is \( A_2 = 2 \, \text{m}^2 \). At time \( t = 0 \), both tanks are empty. Assume that \( r_0(t) \) is held constant at rate \( r_0 \).
What is the maximum value of \( r_0 \) such that neither tank will ever overflow if both tanks start out empty?
\( r_0 = \) ________ m\(^3\)/s
surface_forms:
The figure illustrates a cascaded system of two water tanks. Water flows:
- into the first tank at a rate \( r_0(t) \),
- out of the first tank and into the second at a rate \( r_1(t) \), and
- out of the second tank at a rate \( r_2(t) \).
The rate of flow out of each tank is proportional to the height of the water in that tank: \( r_1(t) = k_1 h_1(t) \) and \( r_2(t) = k_2 h_2(t) \), where \( k_1 \) and \( k_2 \) are each \( 0.2 \, \text{m}^2/\text{second} \). Both tanks have heights of 1 m. The cross-sectional area of tank 1 is \( A_1 = 4 \, \text{m}^2 \) and that of the second tank is \( A_2 = 2 \, \text{m}^2 \). At time \( t = 0 \), both tanks are empty. Assume that \( r_0(t) \) is held constant at rate \( r_0 \).
What is the maximum value of \( r_0 \) such that neither tank will ever overflow if both tanks start out empty?
\( r_0 = \) ________ m\(^3\)/s

normalized_value.title: EEE-Bench 开放题
normalized_value.rewritten_question_text:
The figure illustrates a cascaded system of two water tanks. Water flows:
- into the first tank at a rate \( r_0(t) \),
- out of the first tank and into the second at a rate \( r_1(t) \), and
- out of the second tank at a rate \( r_2(t) \).
The rate of flow out of each tank is proportional to the height of the water in that tank: \( r_1(t) = k_1 h_1(t) \) and \( r_2(t) = k_2 h_2(t) \), where \( k_1 \) and \( k_2 \) are each \( 0.2 \, \text{m}^2/\text{second} \). Both tanks have heights of 1 m. The cross-sectional area of tank 1 is \( A_1 = 4 \, \text{m}^2 \) and that of the second tank is \( A_2 = 2 \, \text{m}^2 \). At time \( t = 0 \), both tanks are empty. Assume that \( r_0(t) \) is held constant at rate \( r_0 \).
What is the maximum value of \( r_0 \) such that neither tank will ever overflow if both tanks start out empty?
\( r_0 = \) ________ m\(^3\)/s
